speaker: interviewer
text: This is the beginning of tape number five. Ben, could you tell us more about your brother returning to Costa Rica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. After fulfilling the promise made to my father that I'm-- I'm always going to be at his side or with him, he-- he wasn't happy being in the United States. More so, his wife, who was really a lady in there, because she had mates-- she never had to do any washing, any ironing, any cleaning, any dressing of the kids, or taking care of the kids. So, of course, she didn't like the United States. I recognized this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one Sunday, after he lived in here about three months, I told him, look. You're spoiled. The life in Costa Rica is much easier for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, yes. It was much easier, he says. And if I would have worked as hard as I'm working here, now, he says, six days a week-- he was working a supermarket as a produce man-- he says, I could have been a wealthy man. Over there, I only worked one day, two days a week. I had enough.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told him, United States is not for you. If you would have come right away here, it would have been different. But you already lived in there, I said. I think you should go back. I said, remember that the promise made to our father was wartimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, and-- and-- and-- and-- you are married. I was already married. In '53, when he came here, I was only married about a year. And I said, we-- we all have our own lives. It's not war anymore, I said. It was a different time and a different life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he, for a change, he listened to me. I mean, and-- and-- and as it turned out, even in the camps, that I was taking care more of him than he was taking care of me. I was younger. I was more daring to take chances. And-- and this is how we survived. So, he listened. Because he also wanted to go back. It was too hard for him in here. And he finally went back to Costa Rica.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and he told me, he says, now I know what life is in the United States, he says, now I'm going to go to work, to get someplace. And he has accomplished an awful lot. He was a good brother. I went on many vacations to Costa Rica. I have never been to Vegas. But have been at least 20 times to Costa Rica. And my wife was there. My kids were there a couple of times. Matter of fact, my youngest daughter had her sweet 16 over there. I think they do it at 15. Though she had her sweet 16. They made her a party that she'll never forget in her life, including mariachis, a whole band singing for her, serenading her at the window. And it was just a-- a beautiful thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had a real wonderful brother. He led a happy life. He-- he worked at one of the-- he purchased one of the main appliances-- appliance stores in Costa Rica. And he has become a distributor for Hotpoint. And it was doing very, very well. And got to be a very wealthy person.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, my brother passed away about five years ago in Costa Rica, and-- but his family is there. He has a son. He has a daughter. He has about eight grandchildren. And they all still live-- not-- I shouldn't say all. The grandchildren have all-- his sons and daughters still live in Costa Rica. But all the grandchildren-- not all-- my niece's grandchildren have all-- all living in the United States. They just-- they were young enough to appreciate what the United States has to offer. And they all in here. They don't want to return. They go back there on vacations. But they-- they all live in here now, in the United States. Now, as I've indicated before, I had a wonderful life. I mean, my life actually began when I met my wife and when we got married. My wife, her name is Esther. And she worked side by side with me all through the years. I'm in the insurance business. I still am in the insurance business. We had three children. My oldest son, Lawrence, we call him Larry. It's Lawrence David. My daughter-- Larry was born in 1955, February 2. My second one was a daughter, Dina Rochell. Larry is named after my father, [NON-ENGLISH], so, translation, Lawrence David. His name is [NON-ENGLISH] David. Excuse me. He couldn't be named [NON-ENGLISH] David. He couldn't be named [NON-ENGLISH] simply because my father-in-law was named [NON-ENGLISH]. So his name is [NON-ENGLISH] David, my father's name. My daughter is named-- Dina is named after my mother and after my sister. Her name is Dina Rochell. And my youngest one is named after a-- on both sides, after our grandmothers. They were both Faiges. And that was my grandmother. So she's named Faye [? Alene. ?] She's married. My son is not married yet. My daughter is in process of getting a divorce. And my youngest one is very happily married to Mark. He's a physician. He's a medical doctor. They have a baby. My daughter Dina has two children, which you'll meet. My-- a boy and a girl. My youngest one, Faye, she has one child, a daughter, named Sarah. She is named after her grandmother, after my wife's mother. In 1994, my wife suddenly took sick. She has never, never before been ill in her life. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only time she was in a hospital is she had her appendix out when she was a youngster, and when she had the children. That was the only time she spent in a hospital. She contracted [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] cancer of the gallbladder. She could tolerate an awful lot of pain. But she had a pain and they discovered it was gallstones. And when they finally took the gallstones out laparoscopically, they also took some biopsies. And the gallbladder was cancerous. And unfortunately, it was already late. She could always tolerate a lot of pain. She never complained. She always said she's of good stock. Her parents were Russian peasants. And she's-- she was never afraid of anything. Upon discovery of this, we immediately went to the Sloan Kettering clinic in New York [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a consultation. Of course, we came back home after the consultation. And they said they would have to operate in order to confirm what they think it is. And after about a week's time, we decided to go back to New York to the Sloan Kettering clinic. And she was operated. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We talked with the doctor beforehand. And I asked the doctor, how long will the operation take? And his words to me were, I hope it will take five hours. That means I can do something for her. If it will be less, he says, then unfortunately, who knows. So, of course, unfortunately, after two hours, I hoped that it's-- he's continuing and doing fine. But he came out and-- afterwards. And he told me that there is-- I couldn't understand how he could say this-- there's almost no hope. Because it's spread a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was only the surgeon. And then, of course, we had to speak with the oncologists. And they told us, well, you might as well go back home. Because New York is very expensive. We have no special treatment for gallbladder cancer because it is-- it is a very, very rare thing. There's only about three cases reported. And we don't have any special things for it. We can take care of, of, of, maybe a uterus. We can take care of breast cancer, he says. But we-- we use the same methods of curing. We came home here. She-- she spoke to about three oncologists at University of Michigan and at Beaumont Hospital. We finally wound up at Harper. She found a lady who says that she can work with her. Because she had a tremendous willpower to live. She could overcome all obstacles. It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she was-- she was everything. Not only to me, I mean, but the kids also. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not only were we just married in the word sense of marriage, but we worked in the same office.

speaker: interviewee
text: People would come to us for advice. How can you stand being 24 hours together and still be the way you are? We were really as we say, a match or a [NON-ENGLISH] which was made in heaven. I mean, we were so compatible, in every respect.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we-- we cherished each other. We-- we respected each other. And-- and our love just grew the-- the longer we were married, the happier we were, the-- the-- the more we were in love. After taking all the chemotherapy treatments, Dr. V.-- his name is a long name. It's a Slavic name. But they're all-- he's referred to as Dr. V. He said to-- he was her doctor. He was nominated, as a matter of fact, by President Clinton to be one of the three research cancer doctors in the United States or in the world. And he was in good hands-- she was in good hands with him. And he said for all intensive purposes, from what-- all the tests which they have taken, that she's cured. There's only one more confirmation we have to have, is go in for exploratory, to see whether it's so. Because every-- all the other vital statistics show that she's cured. And she wasn't afraid of an operation. She said, absolutely. Go in. Well, he called me from the hospital room and he told me that, Mr. Kawer, she's as clean as I am. Of course, we had a happy time crying. And I asked him to repeat this to the kids, which are here, will be here any minute now. And of course, we all had a good cry. She finally-- they brought her up about 5 o'clock into her room. And she was crying from happiness that she's cured. But they said in order to make absolutely, positively sure, they want to give her radiation, 26 treatments of radiation, to make sure that everything else is killed, that they couldn't see with-- with the eyes. We took the radiation. And as a matter of fact, we went to Florida for a wedding, minus one radiation treatment. We were only there about 48 hours. We came back. And of course, she took her last treatment. And everything was fine. We went to Florida for a little rest, for about six weeks. And she was doing fine. Those were like a new lease on life. Those were the happiest days of our life. Not that we weren't happy all the years. We were extremely happy. And then, suddenly, in March-- we came back in about February or so, back from Florida-- and in March, it just started off with a vengeance. She-- the-- the cancer came back. And-- and she was really swelling up. And we took her to the hospital just to be drained. Because she was so big from all the fluid. And, unfortunately, they never released her. He says, I want to keep her in here. And never told me anything. My wife, I guess, knew that she was dying. I never believed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the doctor called me in on a Wednesday. And he says, I'm going to break a promise which I made to your wife. I said, what sort of a promise? He says, I told her I will never tell you. And I said, oh my god. She knew that she was dying? He says, yes. But she made me promise-- she was shielding me all the time, just like in the earlier years.

speaker: interviewee
text: She knew what I went through. And every time something was shown on television about concentration camps or wars, she would always take over the clicker. She'd say, tonight is my night for television. I-- I decide what's to watch. She thought she was protecting me. I knew what she meant by this. But that was fine with me. I mean, and-- and-- and-- my wife was a girl who was born right here in Detroit. She was a product of Detroit. Anyway, when I found this out, of course, I guess, my life was devastated also. And this is already after spending with her, about nine days in the hospital. I slept in the hospital. I had a cot in there, in her room. I was with her. And he came back from his trip, the doctor, and-- he went to a conference, I guess, to Washington-- when he came back, she lost her-- she lost conscious about-- it was on a Friday. She always used to light candles for holidays and for Friday night. I-- I bought a candelabra, and electric one, to the hospital. And she lit the candles. She still knew how to do it. And she still made the blessings with everybody present in there, right this particular night. And my kids blessed the candles too. And my-- my-- my daughters and my granddaughter. My granddaughter, who was by her side all the time. She's only 11 years old. But she was holding her hands while she was dying, as a matter of fact. And that night, that Friday night, which was about July 7, I believe-- Saturday was 10. Saturday was 10. Sunday-- no. It was about July 7, yeah. She died on July 10. That night, she went into a coma. And she never regained consciousness. We were there, all of us, for the next three days, the kids. And Monday night, about 7:30, she went. Rabbi was there. Came in every day. He said, let's prayers. [SOB] He said, read it with her. And she died with-- she had all her loved ones there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All the kids were there. My best friends were there. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Of course, according to our traditions, she-- they did not perform any autopsies on her. And I waited until [SIGHS] the funeral home came to take her away. And we left. It was tough for all of us to accept it. She was the greatest mother. She was the greatest grandmother. Of course, she was a lover, a wife, a partner. [SIGHS] She was a friend to everyone. If she touched someone, I mean, there wasn't a person in this world which wouldn't like her. Of course, we all miss her terribly. [SIGHS] For the next year, according to our tradition, we have to go to services. And we finally had an unveiling, which was June 30 of this year, on a Sunday. And we put a marker on her grave. And we had quite a few people at the unveiling. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And knowing I-- I just had to say a few words in there. I knew it was going to be very hard. But I decided to write something. I never knew I had it in me. But 5:30 that morning, I couldn't sleep. I got up. And I put together something, which I tried to read. And I did read it at the unveiling. I was asked to read this and I gladly will, if I can. In her honor. In her tribute. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] [SIGHS] June 30, 1996, in memory to my beloved wife, Esther. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If I could reverse time, you would forever be mine. If I could hold the tide, you would always be by my side. For just one more glance at you, I would give my life to spend with-- with you. Our love grew steadily, not fast nor slow. But in the later years, our love could melt mountains of snow. Our love was never taken for granted. We worked at it and we meant it. The 43 years of our marriage was like a blink of an eye. I could not forget you, no matter how I try. When I look at my children, I see you. When I look at our grandchildren, I see you. The love you instilled in our children and grandchildren is you, you, and only you. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] No matter the years, no matter the season, you will never be forgotten as long as I can reason. So you see, my love, you will never be forgotten. Instead, you will be remembered and revered always. Thank you for a wonderful life. [CRYING] [SIGHS] Thank you for being my wife. That inscription is on her tombstone, I put down. Again, thank you for a wonderful life. Thank you for being my wife. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Life has to go on, I know, but-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's more than a year now, but it's kind of hard. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] We loved each other. I know I'm not the only one. But I have never, never dreamed that I'd be the one-- that she would be the one that is gonna go first. I was not prepared for it. I guess nobody is ever prepared. [SIGHS] That's about my life up till now. I know I have to go on. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] She's missed. She's missed by the kids, too. We all mourn it in a different way. I know the kids miss her also. [BACKGROUND NOISE] Terribly. And my friends. I got-- I have really wonderful kids. And you'll meet 'em. [SIGHS] And I also have wonderful, wonderful friends. And nieces and nephews, which without them, I don't think I could have made it. My friends were constantly on-- on my case. I mean, they would never leave me alone. My nieces and nephews, especially-- Karen and [? Sid. ?] And their children, just like our children. I mean, they-- they-- they just grew up with us. And-- and-- and-- and-- and-- and it's not just them. But it still continued. I mean, it's-- it's-- it's her nephew. And it's from her side of the family. Without them, I would have been much more-- harder for me to go on. And they still the same, even now. I mean, it hasn't stopped just because she's gone. It's still the same, so-- thank god for great family. And same thing goes with my kids, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ben, what words would you'd like to leave for the world to remember about your experience with the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, for my own-- [SIGHS] you know, we-- we, the survivors-- I'm speaking about myself and maybe I'm speaking for the other ones who are not here anymore. We never talked, or we were not ashamed-- afraid to talk-- because we just couldn't believe what we went through. We couldn't believe it. So how could we make someone else-- other people believe it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, seeing children just taken by the legs and torn apart. Taken by the legs, hit against brick walls. Gassing people, shooting people in front of you. I-- I mean, who would believe it? Mass graves, I mean, with-- with thousands of people in one.

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in that last camp. I used to put them into that grave and just put a little lime over them. So we were just closed-mouthed, that nobody wanted to talk about it. Well, the-- getting fewer and fewer of us left. [BACKGROUND NOISE] It is now-- all this started in about 1939. And-- and-- and-- and that breed is dying out. We were the witnesses. And we just-- just have to tell our stories now. I know I'm not the only one telling this. If we don't-- if you won't learn from history, history has a tendency of repeating itself. And we have seen it in our times even now.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we began to tell our stories. And, of course, I also probably have a selfish reason. My kids, I have never talked with them. I have never sat down, really, explained to them what I went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope to leave some legacy. At least they'll know who I was. And it will be easier from now on, to talk to my kids. When they were growing up, they were asking me what my tattoo means. I just told them it's a number. That's all. When they grew older, of course, they knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing when my grandchildren were growing up. They would ask me, Zaydeh, what's this? And I would just tell them-- because they call me Zaydeh. They don't call me Grandpa. They call me Zaydeh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would-- I would just tell them, well, it's a number. Now, they grew up. They also know. Now I don't-- I wouldn't say lie to them. I don't tell them-- I-- I don't-- I don't avoid the truth. I just tell them how this happened and why this happened. Then we were-- the only reason that we have is because we were Jewish. We didn't do anything wrong. And there was a time with Hitler and the Nazis in the-- in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm just telling the story. Maybe historians can somehow analyze it. And maybe the world will see it and-- and-- and-- and-- and-- and hoping that it will never, never again repeat itself, what we went through. That's it.
